annotations.text: The temporal relationship between the initiation of methotrexate six weeks prior to the onset of oral ulcers and the subsequent eruption is highly suggestive of a drug etiology.

Methotrexate is a well-documented, albeit uncommon, trigger of TEN, particularly in patients with renal impairment, as it is primarily excreted renally and accumulates in the setting of chronic kidney disease.

The fact that the eruption followed the appearance of oral ulcers—a classic prodrome of TEN—strongly favors a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction rather than a direct cytotoxic effect, aligning with the immune pathogenesis of TEN.
However, the differential diagnosis of TEN must also consider other conditions that mimic its presentation.

The hallmark clinical features—rapid progression from erythema and targetoid lesions to confluent epidermal sloughing, mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital), and systemic toxicity—are all present here.
The patient’s underlying medical conditions created a perfect storm for the development and fatal progression of TEN.
ign,” and the histopathology of full-thickness epidermal necrosis with minimal dermal inflammation are not typical of HSV.
The patient’s underlying conditions—uremia, diabetes, steroid use, and chronic inflammation—created a physiological environment in which even a single infection could spiral into systemic collapse.
The presence of mucosal ulceration, the histopathologic findings of full-thickness necrosis, and the absence of any staphylococcal isolate from skin or mucosal sites make SSSS highly unlikely.
The case underscores the fragility of the immunocompromised host and the lethal synergy between drug-induced mucocutaneous injury and nosocomial infection.
Chronic renal failure, particularly with hemodialysis dependence, leads to uremic immune dysfunction, impaired drug clearance, and chronic inflammation.
